patterns.name: Phrasal Verbs Mix-Up
patterns.what: Tends to confuse phrasal verbs that look very similar but have completely different meanings.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: angyunshanariel chose 'taken off' instead of 'taken in' for being tricked by a magician.
angyunshanariel picked 'set off' instead of 'set in' when describing fatigue starting to happen.

patterns.strategic_advice:
When you see a verb followed by a small word like **'in'**, **'off'**, or **'up'**, treat it as a single new word.
- Try replacing the phrase with a simple action word (like 'tricked' for 'taken in') to see if it fits the story.

patterns.name: Missing Sentence Clues
patterns.what: Sometimes picks a vocabulary word that contradicts the helpful hints given in the rest of the sentence.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: angyunshanariel picked 'meager' (small) even though the sentence warned about 'lots of leftovers and wastage'.
angyunshanariel chose 'proposed' instead of 'anticipated' for a delay caused by a sudden flashflood.
angyunshanariel picked 'indignant' instead of 'tenacious' despite the clear clue that the opponent was 'never giving up'.

patterns.strategic_advice:
When you see a blank, look for the **'because'** or **'due to'** part of the sentence.
- Highlight the clue words (like 'leftovers' or 'never giving up') before choosing your answer.
- Check if your chosen word matches the feeling of those clues!

patterns.name: Which vs Whose Confusion
patterns.what: Occasionally mixes up 'which' and 'whose' when describing a part that belongs to an object.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: angyunshanariel picked 'which' instead of 'whose' when talking about the windows that belonged to the old house.
angyunshanariel made the same slip with the old house's broken windows.

patterns.strategic_advice:
When you see a blank right after a noun (like 'house'), check the very next word.
- If it's something that belongs to the noun (like 'windows'), use **'whose'**.
- If it's an action or a description, use **'which'**.

patterns.name: Word Partnership Slips
patterns.what: Sometimes chooses a word that makes logical sense but isn't the natural pairing usually used in English.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: angyunshanariel picked 'waived' instead of the correct English phrase 'stripped of' for losing a medal.
angyunshanariel chose 'promoted' instead of 'raised' when talking about boosting the class's morale.

patterns.strategic_advice:
When two words seem to fit the meaning perfectly, read the sentence aloud with each option.
- Often, your ears will catch the **natural pairing** (like 'raised morale' or 'stripped of').
- Pay special attention to the little prepositions (like **'of'**) that come right after the blank!
